title: Audience Hall Drapes
desc: The black drapes in the audience hall hang for intimidation, privacy, and echoes, and today half of them are doing all three badly.

steps.text:
The audience hall in Gothos Tower is long, narrow, and set up to make visitors feel that they have entered too far to leave with dignity. The black drapes along its walls are a large part of that work. They swallow drafts, hide side passages, soften the wrong echoes, and frame the right silences. This morning a shift of air from the upper vents has left one side uneven and the rear curtains twisted into shapes that suggest neglect rather than design.

The hall keeper stands under a ladder with a pouch of weights at her belt. "Rehang the side drapes if you want the obvious job," she says. "Or reset the rear curtains and tiebacks if you prefer the subtler one. Both matter. Intimidation is architecture with maintenance."

options.label: Rehang the side drapes along the hall wall
Reset the rear curtains and hidden tiebacks

steps.text:
The side drapes are heavier than they look and determined to remind you of it. You climb, unhook the twisted top rings, and draw each panel back into even folds so the fabric falls in long straight lines instead of bunched black resentments. Once the weights are corrected, the wall regains its intended shape: not soft, exactly, but controlled. The hall narrows again in the eye, and the pillars behind the cloth return to implication rather than evidence.

From the threshold, the keeper studies the new fall of the fabric and the way it shortens the room without changing a single stone. "Better," she says. "Guests should feel watched, not invited." In Gothos Tower that apparently counts as hospitality.

options.label: Step down from the ladder and leave the hall properly severe

steps.text:
The rear curtains control what happens after a visitor looks toward the dais and notices there is more darkness behind it than the room should reasonably possess. You retie the concealed pulls, seat the bottom weights, and draw the folds into a clean vertical line that hides the service slit and the narrow speaker's gap behind them. Once reset, the curtains stop suggesting a clumsy cover-up and begin doing what they were made to do: make the back of the hall look deeper than the architecture allows.

The keeper crosses to the entrance and gives a short clap. The sound dies where it should and returns where she wants it to, colder and smaller. She seems satisfied by that. "Good," she says. "If a room is going to frighten someone, it should never sound accidental."

options.label: Gather the spare weights and leave the rear wall composed
